title: China Trade Relations Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill stops giving China special trade benefits that make it cheaper and easier to buy things from China. China would have to pay higher taxes on goods they sell to America. To get the special trade benefits back, China must follow rules about treating people fairly, stop forcing people to work against their will, protect people in Tibet, stop taking organs from prisoners, and make other changes. The bill makes trade with China more expensive until China improves how it treats its people.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Rules, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as
subjects: Presidents and presidential powers, Vice Presidents

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the China Trade Relations Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Withdrawal of Normal Trade Relations Treatment from the People's Republic of China
sections.text: Starting on the day this law is passed, the United States will change how it trades with China. Normal trade relations treatment means giving a country the same low taxes on goods that we give to our closest trading partners. This law says China will no longer get this special treatment. From now on, if China wants normal trade relations treatment again, it must follow the same old rules that were in place before China joined the World Trade Organization. There was also a special permission called waiver authority that let the President give China trade benefits even if China did not meet certain requirements. This law brings back that waiver authority for ninety days after this law is passed.

sections.title: Section 3 Expansion of Bases of Ineligibility of People's Republic of China for Normal Trade Relations
sections.text: This section changes an existing trade law to add more reasons why China cannot get normal trade relations treatment. The law now covers not just freedom of emigration but also human rights issues. China will not be able to get normal trade relations treatment if the President decides that China is doing any of these things. China must not block people from leaving the country or fail to promote freedom of emigration. China must follow a nineteen ninety two agreement about not using prison labor to make products for export. China must follow International Labour Organization standards for workers. China must not operate vocational training and education centers or other camps where people are held against their will. China must not intimidate or harass Chinese people living in other countries. China must protect Tibet's religious and cultural heritage. China must not steal economic secrets from the United States including taking intellectual property from American people and companies. China must also make significant progress on following the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. China must stop using torture. China must release people who are in prison just for peacefully expressing their political or religious beliefs including beliefs about Hong Kong and Taiwan independence. China must treat prisoners humanely and let international humanitarian and human rights organizations visit prisons. China must allow international radio and television broadcasts into the country. China must stop harvesting organs from prisoners without their permission. China must stop forcing people to have abortions or sterilization procedures. The President must write a report to Congress twice a year on June thirtieth and December thirty first explaining whether China is following these rules. The President can waive these requirements for twelve months at a time if the President believes this will help achieve these goals and if China promises to improve. Congress can reject the President's decision to extend a waiver by passing a joint resolution of disapproval. If Congress passes this resolution and overrides a Presidential veto, the waiver authority ends sixty days after the resolution becomes law.
